title: How to Create a Wealth Consciousness That Includes Your Emotions
body:
How to Create a Wealth Consciousness That Includes Your Emotions

Truth is, you're probably fighting with your money. Not with your bank account balance, though that's often where the battle shows up. No, you're fighting with the stories you tell yourself about money. You're fighting with the shame, the fear, the guilt, the anxiety that money stirs up in your gut. You're fighting with the deep, unconscious beliefs that dictate your financial reality far more than any budgeting app ever could. This isn't just about making more money or saving more money. It's about healing your relationship with it. It's about recognizing that money isn't some cold, hard, external thing. It's deeply, intricately woven into your inner world. What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? Think about it. Money doesn't lie. It reflects back to you every hidden fear, every unacknowledged desire, every unresolved trauma. It shows you exactly where you're holding back, where you're playing small, where you're avoiding discomfort. It's a mirror. And sometimes, looking in that mirror feels unbearable. That's why so many of us avoid it. We push it away, distract ourselves, pretend it's not a big deal. But it is a big deal. Because when you ignore your money story, you're ignoring a huge part of yourself. Money is just energy with a story attached to it. Your story. And that story isn't just made of thoughts. It's made of feelings. Big, messy, uncomfortable feelings. You can't separate the two. To try and build wealth consciousness without acknowledging and integrating your emotional world is like trying to build a house on quicksand. It just won't hold.

Your Emotional Bank Account: More Than Just Numbers

You've heard of emotional intelligence, right? Well, there's an emotional financial intelligence too. It's the ability to understand and manage your feelings around money, and to use that understanding to make better decisions. Most of us operate from a place of emotional ignorance when it comes to our finances. We react. We panic. We spend impulsively when we're stressed or sad. We hoard when we're scared. We avoid looking at our statements when we feel shame. Sound familiar? This isn't about being "good" or "bad" with money. It's about being human with money. Brené Brown's work on shame and vulnerability is incredibly relevant here. Money is one of the last great taboos. We'll talk about sex, politics, even our deepest fears, before we'll openly discuss our salaries, our debts, or our financial insecurities. Why? Because the shame around money is crushing. It tells us we're not enough, we're failures, we're irresponsible. It makes us hide. And hiding never leads to freedom or abundance. It only perpetuates the cycle of fear and scarcity. To create a true wealth consciousness, you have to be willing to get vulnerable with your money. You have to be willing to look at the parts of your financial life that make you squirm. You have to be willing to feel the feelings that come up. This isn't about blaming yourself. It's about observing, with compassion. It's about becoming a curious detective of your own inner world. What triggers your financial anxiety? What makes you feel deprived? What stories did you inherit about money from your family? These aren't just intellectual questions. They're felt experiences. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's in your body. It's in your nervous system. It's a primal response. When you feel financially insecure, your body doesn't know the difference between not having enough money and being chased by a saber-toothed tiger. It goes into fight, flight, or freeze. That's why you can feel physically sick when you think about your debt, or why you might avoid opening bills altogether. Your body is trying to protect you, but in doing so, it often keeps you stuck.

The Unseen Forces: Trauma and Money

This might feel heavy, but it's crucial. Our relationship with money is often deeply intertwined with past trauma, big T and little t. Childhood experiences, societal conditioning, generational patterns - they all leave their imprint. If you grew up in scarcity, you might hoard money, even when you have enough, out of a deep-seated fear of lack. If you experienced financial instability in your family, you might unconsciously sabotage your own financial success because prosperity feels unfamiliar or even dangerous. Know what I mean? Consider the work of Bessel van der Kolk in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). He shows how trauma isn't just a mental event; it's stored in our bodies, impacting our physiology, our emotional regulation, and our behavior. This absolutely applies to money. Financial trauma can manifest as chronic stress, anxiety attacks when checking bank balances, compulsive spending as a way to self-soothe, or an inability to save even when you desperately want to. Healing this isn't about intellectualizing it. It's about feeling it. It's about creating a safe space within yourself to acknowledge these deep-seated fears and patterns. This isn't a quick fix. It's a journey of self-discovery and self-compassion. You're not broken. You're just carrying old wounds that are impacting your present reality. And the good news is, you can heal them. Geneen Roth, in her book "Lost and Found," talks about how our relationship with food often mirrors our relationship with money. Both are primal resources, both are often used to numb or control, and both reveal our deepest insecurities. When you start to pay attention to your emotional patterns around one, you'll start to see them in the other. It's all connected.

Bari Tessler and the Art of Money: Feeling Your Way Through

Bari Tessler, author of "The Art of Money," champions an approach that's all about bringing awareness and compassion to your financial life. She doesn't just talk about budgets and spreadsheets, though those are important tools. She emphasizes the emotional aspect, the "money date" where you sit down with your finances and your feelings. It's about feeling your feelings, rather than trying to outsmart them or suppress them. Imagine sitting down with your bank statements. Instead of immediately going into panic or judgment, you take a deep breath. You notice the sensations in your body. Is there tightness in your chest? A knot in your stomach? What emotions are bubbling up? Fear? Anger? Resentment? Sadness? Don't push them away. Just acknowledge them. "Oh, there's fear. I feel fear when I look at this number." This simple act of acknowledgment is revolutionary. It creates space between you and the emotion. It gives you agency. This isn't about letting your emotions dictate your decisions. It's about letting them inform your decisions. When you understand why you feel a certain way about money, you can then choose how to respond, rather than simply reacting from an unconscious place. This is where true wealth consciousness begins to form. It's not about being emotionless. It's about being emotionally intelligent. Krishnamurti spoke about "choiceless awareness," the act of observing without judgment. Apply that to your money emotions. Just observe. Without labeling them as good or bad. Without trying to change them immediately. Just notice what's there. This practice is incredibly powerful for rewiring your brain's relationship with money. It helps you see that you are not your emotions. You are the one experiencing them.

Practical Steps to Emotional Financial Integration

Okay, so how do you actually do this? It sounds abstract, but it's incredibly practical. 1. **The Money Check-In:** This is your daily or weekly ritual. It's not just checking your bank balance. It's checking in with *yourself* about your money. * Find a quiet moment. * Take a few deep breaths. * Ask yourself: "How am I feeling about money right now?" * Don't overthink it. Just let the feelings come. Write them down. * Notice any physical sensations. * Don't judge. Just observe. "I'm feeling anxious about my credit card bill." "I'm feeling excited about a potential new income stream." * This builds your emotional muscle around money. 2. **Identify Your Money Stories:** We all have them. "Money is evil." "I'll never have enough." "Rich people are greedy." "I don't deserve wealth." Where did these stories come from? Your parents? Society? A past experience? Write them down. Once you see them, you can begin to question them. Are they actually true? Or are they just old narratives running on a loop? 3. **Practice Mindful Spending:** Before you buy something, pause. Ask yourself: "Why am I buying this? What emotion am I trying to satisfy or avoid?" Is it genuine need? Joy? Or is it boredom, stress, or a desire for external validation? This isn't about deprivation. It's about conscious choice. It's about aligning your spending with your true values, not just your impulses. 4. **Acknowledge Your Shame and Vulnerability:** This is a big one. As Brené Brown teaches in The Gifts of Imperfection (paid link), vulnerability is the birthplace of connection and courage. Talk about your money fears with a trusted friend, therapist, or coach. Just saying it out loud can break the power of shame. You're not alone in your struggles. We're all in this messy human experience together. 5. **Reframe Scarcity to Sufficiency:** This isn't about pretending you have more than you do. It's about acknowledging what you *do* have. Practice gratitude for the money that comes in, no matter how small. Focus on the feeling of "enough" rather than constantly chasing "more." This shifts your energetic vibration around money. 6. **Educate Yourself, Emotionally:** Read books like The Psychology of Money (paid link). It's not just about financial literacy; it's about understanding the human biases and emotional drivers behind our financial decisions. The more you understand, the less control your unconscious emotions have over you.

Beyond the Numbers: Your Inner Wealth

Ultimately, wealth consciousness isn't just about the number in your bank account. It's about your internal state. It's about feeling secure, abundant, and at peace, regardless of external circumstances. It's about recognizing that your true wealth lies within you - your resilience, your creativity, your capacity for love and joy. Money is a tool. It's a resource. It's a reflection. But it's not the source of your worth. When you heal your emotional relationship with money, you free up incredible amounts of energy. Energy that was previously tied up in fear, anxiety, and avoidance. That freed-up energy can then be directed towards creating the life you truly desire, including your financial life. You become an intentional creator, rather than a reactive victim of circumstances. Does that land? This isn't about manifesting millions overnight. It's about cultivating a deep, abiding sense of inner security and abundance that then naturally attracts more resources into your life. It's about realizing that you are inherently worthy, regardless of your net worth. And from that place of worthiness, everything shifts.

FAQ

What if my emotions about money are really negative and overwhelming?

It's completely normal for money to bring up intense emotions. The key is to not suppress them or judge yourself for having them. Start small. Acknowledge the feeling without judgment. "I feel overwhelmed." "I feel panicked." Just naming it can create a little space. If it feels too much to handle alone, consider seeking support from a therapist or financial coach who understands the emotional aspects of money. You don't have to carry this burden by yourself.

How long does it take to heal my relationship with money?

There's no fixed timeline for this kind of inner work. It's a journey, not a destination. You'll have breakthroughs, and you'll have moments where old patterns resurface. The goal isn't perfection, but progress and consistent awareness. Every time you consciously choose to acknowledge your feelings and question your money stories, you're making progress. Be patient and compassionate with yourself. It took a lifetime to build these patterns; it will take time to gently unwind them.

Is this just about feeling good, or will it actually help me make more money?

This isn't just about feeling good, though that's a wonderful side effect! When you heal your emotional relationship with money, you remove the unconscious blocks that prevent you from attracting and receiving abundance. Fear, shame, and scarcity consciousness can unconsciously sabotage your efforts to earn, save, and invest. By addressing these emotional roots, you create an internal environment that is more open to wealth. You'll make more conscious decisions, take more aligned actions, and stop self-sabotaging. It absolutely impacts your external financial reality.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover how to build a wealth consciousness that embraces your emotions. Learn to understand and work with your financial fears, shame, and anxieties.
author: Kalesh
